title: RA.1026 Carl Craig, Moodymann & Mike Banks

translation.content: "Let's just go through some shit, let's see what we got here." In that unmistakable drawl, Moodymann opens RA.1026—and from there, you know you're in good hands. If you've done your homework, you'll know Mike Banks, Carl Craig and Moodymann are artists of the utmost standing. As producers and founders of the influential labels Underground Resistance, Planet E and Mahogani Music respectively, their personal and curatorial catalogues have shaped electronic music in immeasurably profound ways, from Moodymann's 2004 LP Black Mahogani and Craig's era-defining Junior Boys and Theo Parrish remixes, to Banks's uncompromising output as Underground Resistance. But the records are only part of it. All three are proof that you can build something lasting without corporate backing, that creative freedom is a discipline as much as a right. Through their work, house and techno became more than music; it became a vehicle for resistance, identity and pride. And that legacy still reverberates through the global underground today. Recorded live at Movement in Detroit last year, RA.1026 is a rare document of three electronic icons. Here's Mike Banks on keys, Carl Craig on the decks and Moodymann on the mic, weaving through Motor City staples, '80s classics and deep-cut curveballs—standouts include a spellbinding live take on "The Final Frontier" and a reimagined version of "Knights Of The Jaguar." As Black History Month continues in the US, the mix feels especially momentous, giving series debuts to Banks and Moodymann, two of Detroit's most vital and elusive figures. (Craig's entry, #200, landed in March 2010.) Coming in at just under two hours, RA.1026 isn't about pristine transitions or tidy narrative arcs. It's about chemistry. Shared history. Timeless records. The electricity that sparks when friends—and legends, no less—play for each other as much as for the crowd. - Fahad Akinsanya The interview below is with Carl Craig. What have you been up to recently? Man, I've been scrolling on Instagram for God knows how long. The times are just crazy right now. I guess I use it like when I used to watch TV as a kid—you'd just sit there and whatever came on, you'd watch it, or you'd keep paging through the channels. Of course, I can't be sitting there thinking, "Okay, I'm gonna make music the whole time." Yeah, I'm gonna do that—but not every single moment. The interesting thing about scrolling is that I do find things that come across my feed that actually make sense, or that I find inspiration from, which is great. It's all Black vinyl—February is Black History Month over here—and that's something I've been doing since Covid. It's good to see posts around Black history in the US, because there's always something new I didn't realise before. Inventions, social circumstances, people bringing things to light. So yeah… what have I been doing lately? I've been on Instagram. This mix was recorded at Movement last year. Can you tell us the idea behind all three of you coming together for the set? We've known each other for a long time. It was monumental for all three of us to collaborate like that. With Mike and me, we go pretty straight ahead—he fits into a more techno-centric set. So it's great when Kenny (AKA Moodymann) comes in because his spectrum can go through electro and all these different things. It really caught fire when Mike started doing his gospel piano thing. I think we went from a John Collins track, and then Kenny started going, and it just locked in. That's when it really took off. How would you describe the state of Black dance music in 2026, and who are some of its brightest new talents? Black dance music right now—it's hard for me to make a clear comment because I'm in Detroit, and London has its own thing, Bristol has its thing, Manchester. Everybody's doing their own thing. The guys from here keep going with the mission. Kai Alce sent me a bunch of stuff from his label recently. There's a Reggie Dokes record I really like, and also his record with Ash Lauryn. Both of them will be on future editions of All Black Vinyl. It's great to see people I've known for a long time still pushing themselves musically. And of course, the younger generation—Kyle Hall is still doing great stuff. FunkinEven is still hitting. Seven Davis Jr. is still hitting. Seth Troxler is coming from another direction, which is fantastic. And we could definitely go into the African stuff—one of the guys I like a lot is Mr. Joe. What he does reminds me a little of what Lil' Louis was doing in the late '80s and early '90s, like "French Kiss," or what was happening with acid house with DJ Pierre and Armando and those guys. So creatively, it's strong. But I honestly believe it's still being overshadowed. When people think of electronic music on a large level, they're not really considering how much of it comes from Black music. The festival image—the DJs people stand in the middle of the crowd filming—often doesn't reflect that history. I would love to see that shift, in terms of the image people get when they think of a DJ, house music or techno. I think it's still misrepresented. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? The world needs to pay attention to the systematic and collaborative disenfranchisement that's happening right now. It's happening over here in the US with race, and it spreads. What's happening here isn't nearly as crazy as what's happening in Palestine, but things are going in a direction that feels dangerous. There's a saying, "when the US sneezes, the rest of the world catches a cold." I want people to take what's happening seriously, because it doesn't stay contained. It spreads.
